summary: Gruber analyzes Instagram's radical icon and UI redesign, acknowledging the company's deliberate approach while questioning whether the new rainbow gradient icon goes too far from the original brand. He appreciates the stripped-down, black-and-white in-app UI changes but finds a disconnect between the colorful new icon and the monochrome app interior. He notes that negative reactions to logo changes are inevitable and often driven by loss of familiarity rather than genuine aesthetic judgment, curating a range of industry reactions that illustrate this dynamic.
key_points: Instagram is a deliberate company that stuck with skeuomorphism long after the flat design trend, making this radical change clearly intentional rather than impulsive
The new icon feels like an entirely new brand rather than an evolution of the old one, which Gruber sees as potentially the wrong move
The in-app changes to black-and-white UI that lets user content provide the color are well-considered, but create a disconnect with the colorful icon
Most negative reactions to logo changes are about the loss of familiarity itself, not genuine aesthetic critique — if users could vote, no popular app would ever get a new icon
The new icon is recognizable as a camera but lacks the distinctive elements needed to be recognized specifically as Instagram
money_quotes: Even if you disagree with the direction in which they've taken their brand today, do not think for a second that they did this on a whim.
It looks and feels like an altogether new brand for Instagram, not an update or refresh of their old brand — and I'm not convinced that was the right move.
Some people will genuinely dislike any new icon; it's the change itself they dislike — the loss of something familiar.
If you allowed users to vote on it, no popular app would ever get a new icon.
Most people comment on logo launches as if they're judging a diving competition when they should be judging a swimming competition.

This is not to say it's a bad-looking icon, no… as far as camera icons go, this is quite lovely and has the minimal amount of elements necessary to be recognized as a camera BUT not the minimal amount of elements necessary to be recognized as Instagram.
key_insight: Instagram's new icon succeeds as a camera glyph but fails as a brand evolution because it severs too many ties to the original identity, making it feel like a new brand rather than a refresh.
